title: Joojeh Kebab (Persian Saffron Curd Chicken)

ingredients.name: bone-in chicken pieces or boneless thighs
saffron strands
visual_checks: steep in 3 tbsp warm water for 10 minutes
ingredients.notes: steep in 3 tbsp warm water for 10 minutes

subs.name: a generous pinch of turmeric plus 1 tsp rose water for a hint of fragrance
subs.notes: not identical but gives a golden colour and perfume

subs.name: 3 tbsp thick buttermilk or 2 tbsp cream
subs.notes: curd's lactic acid is key for tenderising

ingredients.name: onion, grated (juice included)
visual_checks: grating releases enzymes that tenderise the chicken
ingredients.notes: grating releases enzymes that tenderise the chicken

subs.name: butter — equal quantity
subs.notes: butter browns faster; watch the heat

ingredients.name: lemon wedges and grilled tomato, to serve

steps.text: Crush the saffron strands lightly and steep in 3 tablespoons of warm water for 10 minutes until the liquid is a deep amber colour.
Combine the saffron water, grated onion (with its juice), curd, lemon juice, black pepper, turmeric and salt in a bowl. Mix well into a smooth yellow marinade.
Score the chicken pieces with a knife to let the marinade penetrate. Add to the marinade and coat well. Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours — 4–6 hours gives a more deeply flavoured result.
Bring the chicken to room temperature 20 minutes before cooking. Heat a grill pan, tawa or the grill to high — it should be very hot before the chicken goes on.

Cook the chicken on high heat for 6–7 minutes per side for bone-in pieces, or 4–5 minutes per side for boneless thighs. Baste generously with melted ghee or butter each time you turn. The chicken is done when deeply golden with some char and the juices run clear when pierced.
Serve with Persian rice or flatbread, halved tomatoes grilled alongside (baste them with saffron butter too) and lemon wedges. A bowl of fresh herbs and onion rings is the traditional accompaniment.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: saffron → a generous pinch of turmeric plus 1 tsp rose water for a hint of fragrance (not identical but gives a golden colour and perfume); curd → 3 tbsp thick buttermilk or 2 tbsp cream (curd's lactic acid is key for tenderising); ghee → butter — equal quantity (butter browns faster; watch the heat)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: All ingredients are standard Indian pantry items. Marinate and cook on a cast-iron tawa on high flame, or thread onto skewers and grill over a gas burner directly. Saffron is available at Indian spice shops — a small pinch goes a long way. The marinade is very similar to Indian dahi murgh.
